title: SEGMENT 12 — The Hubrecht biobank and the organoid commons

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — The Hubrecht biobank and the organoid commons
Let me pause here and tell you about a thing that I think is genuinely undersung in the public conversation about organoids.
It's the existence of a large and growing biobank infrastructure — a network of repositories of organoid lines that researchers can access for study.
The most prominent of these is at the Hubrecht Institute in Utrecht, where Hans Clevers' group has built, over roughly a decade, an enormous collection of patient-derived organoids.
The biobank now contains thousands of organoid lines spanning many cancer types: colorectal, breast, pancreatic, prostate, liver, bladder, lung, head-and-neck, and more.
Each line is derived from a specific patient's tumor (or, for normal-tissue lines, a specific donor's healthy tissue).
Each line has been genomically characterized — whole-genome sequencing, RNA sequencing, sometimes proteomics.
Each line is frozen down and replenishable.
And each line is, in principle, available to academic researchers who can demonstrate a valid scientific use.
Think about what this means.
If you're a researcher who wants to ask, "What makes some pancreatic cancers respond to drug X and others not?
" — you don't have to recruit a hundred pancreatic cancer patients (which would take years and involve enormous ethical and logistical infrastructure).
You can request, say, 50 different pancreatic cancer organoids from the biobank, treat them with drug X, identify which ones respond, and then look at the genomics to find the pattern.
You can do, in months, the kind of correlative analysis that would take a clinical trial years.

If you're a pharma company developing a new targeted therapy, you can test it against the biobank's panel of, say, colorectal cancer organoids carrying different driver mutations, and predict in advance which patient subgroups are most likely to benefit.
This informs trial design — you can enrich your clinical trial for the patients most likely to respond, increasing your chance of seeing a positive signal.

If you're a basic biologist studying tumor heterogeneity, you can study how organoids derived from different regions of the same tumor differ from each other, capturing intratumor heterogeneity in a way that wasn't possible before.
The biobank is, in essence, a library of patient-derived organoids spanning many cancers, and it functions as a research commons that anyone in the academic world can draw on.
It's a deliberate scientific public good.
Hans Clevers and his colleagues built it, and continue to expand it, because they believe that having this resource shared widely accelerates research far more than hoarding it.
This pattern — open biobanks, shared organoid lines, deposited reagents that anyone can request — is becoming the norm in the field.
There are now organoid biobanks for cystic fibrosis (in the Netherlands), for inflammatory bowel disease, for various rare diseases.

There are organoid lines deposited in repositories like the European Collection of Authenticated Cell Cultures (ECACC) and the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC) and similar Japanese and Chinese registries.
The shared resource model that started with plasmids in the Addgene era and continued with iPSC repositories is now playing out for organoids.
It's worth pausing to appreciate this.
A patient consents to donate a piece of their tumor at the time of surgery.
The tumor gets grown into an organoid line.
That line gets deposited in a biobank.
Years later, researchers around the world are using that line to test new drugs, identify biomarkers, understand disease mechanism.
The patient may have long since died from their cancer, but their tumor — preserved, replicated, studied — continues to contribute to the science that helps the next generation of patients.
There's something quietly humane about this.
The patient becomes, posthumously, a contributor to the research effort that will help others.
There are, of course, ethical and consent questions involved in this kind of biobanking.
Patients need to be informed and consent to the use of their tissue for research.
The use needs to be limited to research purposes, not commercial sale of identifiable material.
The genetic information embedded in the organoid line — which carries the patient's full genome — needs to be protected.
These issues have largely been worked out by the institutional biobanks, with carefully designed consent forms and governance frameworks.
But they remain alive considerations as the biobanks grow and as the kinds of questions researchers want to ask of them expand.
The big takeaway: biological research is, increasingly, a commons-driven enterprise.

The most valuable resources — organoid lines, iPSC lines, plasmid libraries, antibody panels — are shared across institutions, and the resulting scientific velocity is much greater than it would be if each lab had to generate everything from scratch.
Organoid biobanks are one of the more recent and powerful examples of this commons-building, and they're a quiet revolution in how clinical and basic research interact.
